frame_label: Women's Safety as Her Own Responsibility
generated_comment:
Spoken conversation:
A: Sir, my daughter wants to come back late from her friend's birthday.
Deepak: Dekho, aajkal zamaana theek nahi hai. Ladki ko itni raat tak bahar rehne dena practical nahi hai, better hai family ke saath aaye ya cab ka live location share kare.
A: But boys are also out late.
Deepak: Arre, main blame nahi kar raha, bas safety ke liye bol raha hoon. Jo bhi incident hota hai na, pehla sawaal wahi hota hai ki itni raat ko akeli kyun thi.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is misogynistic because it frames women's safety as their own responsibility and normalizes restricting girls rather than questioning male violence. It uses protective language, practical-advice framing, and post-assault victim questioning ('itni raat ko akeli kyun thi').
original_frame: Gendered Division of Chores via Practicality
persona_text: You are Deepak. Age 41. Married. A man in Pune. Your wife Sheetal works as a nurse on rotating shifts including night shifts. On the days she works nights she sleeps during the day. You are alone at home with your two children aged 7 and 4 on those days and you manage everything Ñ school pickup, cooking, cleaning, bedtime. Your male colleague just asked you how you manage and whether it is difficult. You are answering him. Your answer reveals that you see your management of the household on those specific days as a heroic and exceptional effort rather than as a normal division of domestic responsibility Ñ and that on the days Sheetal is not on night shift the assumption immediately reverts to her.
